title: Core Java Quick Reference
description: JVM internals, OOP, Collections, Concurrency — the bedrock of every Java interview.
bodyHtml:
📦 Collections Complexity
CollectionGetAddRemoveSearch
ArrayListO(1)O(1)~O(n)O(n)
LinkedListO(n)O(1)O(1)O(n)
HashMapO(1)~O(1)~O(1)~O(1)~
TreeMapO(log n)O(log n)O(log n)O(log n)
HashSet—O(1)~O(1)~O(1)~
🔐 HashMap Internal Flow
1. key.hashCode() → hash value
2. index = hash & (capacity-1)
3. Bucket found → check collision
4. Java 8+: LinkedList → TreeMap when >8 entries in bucket (treeification threshold)
5. Load factor 0.75 → resize at 75% full

Key requirement: Override both hashCode() and equals() for custom keys!
🔄 Thread States (Lifecycle)
NEW → Thread created, not started
RUNNABLE → Running or ready to run
BLOCKED → Waiting for monitor lock
WAITING → wait() / join() with no timeout
TIMED_WAITING → sleep() / wait(timeout)
TERMINATED → Execution complete
🏗️ OOP Pillars Summary
Encapsulation — Hide data with private fields + public getters/setters
Inheritance — extends keyword; Java = single inheritance only
Polymorphism — Overloading (compile-time) + Overriding (runtime)
Abstraction — Abstract classes (partial) + Interfaces (full)

Key rule: Interfaces support multiple inheritance in Java via implements
🧵 Concurrency Quick Reference
synchronized
Method/block lock. One thread at a time. Use on shared mutable state.
volatile
Ensures visibility across threads. No atomicity. Use for flags, not counters.
AtomicInteger
CAS operations. Lock-free. Better than synchronized for simple counters.
ReentrantLock
More flexible than synchronized. Supports tryLock(), fairness, multiple conditions.
ConcurrentHashMap
Segment-level locking. Multiple threads can read/write different buckets concurrently.
ExecutorService
Thread pool management. Prefer over raw Thread creation. Use Executors.newFixedThreadPool().

title: Java 8 Quick Reference
description: Lambda, Streams, Optional, Date-Time API — the most asked Java 8 features in interviews.
bodyHtml:
⚡ Lambda Syntax
// No args
() -> System.out.println("Hello")

// One arg (parens optional)
x -> x * 2

// Multiple args
(a, b) -> a + b

// Block body
(a, b) -> {
    int sum = a + b;
    return sum;
}

// Method reference
String::toUpperCase
list::add
Integer::new
🌊 Stream Pipeline
List<String> result = employees.stream()
  // Intermediate (lazy)
  .filter(e -> e.getSalary() > 50000)
  .map(Employee::getName)
  .sorted()
  .distinct()
  .limit(10)
  // Terminal (eager - triggers execution)
  .collect(Collectors.toList());

// Other terminals:
// .count(), .findFirst(), .anyMatch()
// .reduce(), .forEach(), .toMap()
🎭 Functional Interfaces (4 Core)
Predicate<T> → T → boolean (test, filter)
Function<T,R> → T → R (transform, map)
Consumer<T> → T → void (forEach, side effects)
Supplier<T> → () → T (factory, lazy evaluation)

BiXxx variants take 2 args: BiFunction<T,U,R>
🛡️ Optional — Null Safety
// Creation
Optional.of(value)        // throws NPE if null
Optional.ofNullable(val)  // null-safe
Optional.empty()          // explicitly empty

// Usage
opt.isPresent()           // check
opt.get()                 // get (throws if empty)
opt.orElse(defaultVal)    // safe get
opt.orElseGet(supplier)   // lazy default
opt.orElseThrow()         // throw if empty
opt.map(fn)               // transform if present
opt.filter(pred)          // filter

// Store null in Optional? NO!
// Optional.of(null) → NullPointerException
📅 Date-Time API (java.time)
LocalDate
Date only (2024-01-15). No time, no timezone.
LocalTime
Time only (10:30:00). No date, no timezone.
LocalDateTime
Date + Time. No timezone. Use for local business logic.
ZonedDateTime
Date + Time + Zone. Use for cross-timezone APIs.
Why new API? Old java.util.Date/Calendar were mutable, not thread-safe, poorly designed. java.time is immutable, thread-safe, fluent, and ISO-8601 compliant by default.

title: Spring Boot Quick Reference
description: Annotations, DI, REST, Security, Caching, Actuator — everything Spring in one place.
bodyHtml:
🏷️ Core Annotations
@SpringBootApplication = @Configuration + @EnableAutoConfiguration + @ComponentScan
@RestController = @Controller + @ResponseBody
@Service → Business logic layer
@Repository → Data layer + exception translation
@Component → Generic bean
@Bean → Method produces a bean (inside @Configuration)
@Autowired → Inject dependency (prefer constructor injection)
@Value → Inject property values
🌐 REST Mapping Annotations
@GetMapping → Read resource
@PostMapping → Create resource
@PutMapping → Replace entire resource (idempotent)
@PatchMapping → Partial update
@DeleteMapping → Delete resource
@PathVariable → /users/{id} — required by default
@RequestParam → /users?role=admin — optional by default
@RequestBody → JSON body → Java object (POST/PUT)
@ResponseEntity → Full control over HTTP response (status + headers + body)
🔒 Bean Scopes
singleton (default) — One instance per Spring container. Shared everywhere.
prototype — New instance every time it's requested.
request — One per HTTP request (web-aware only).
session — One per HTTP session (web-aware only).
application — One per ServletContext.

Senior tip: Injecting a prototype bean into a singleton? Use @Lookup or ObjectFactory — @Autowired won't get a new prototype each time.
⚡ Caching
// Enable caching
@EnableCaching  // on @Configuration class

// Cache result on first call
@Cacheable("users")
public User getUserById(Long id) { ... }

// Update cache after method execution
@CachePut(value="users", key="#user.id")
public User updateUser(User user) { ... }

// Remove from cache
@CacheEvict(value="users", key="#id")
public void deleteUser(Long id) { ... }

// Custom key
@Cacheable(value="users", key="#id + '_' + #role")
🔧 Actuator Endpoints
/actuator/health → App health status (UP/DOWN)
/actuator/info → App info (version, build)
/actuator/metrics → JVM, HTTP, DB metrics
/actuator/env → Environment properties
/actuator/loggers → View/change log levels live
/actuator/beans → All Spring beans

Senior tip: Expose actuator via separate port in prod. Secure /actuator/env and /actuator/beans behind admin auth.
🌍 Profiles
Config files: application-{profile}.properties

Activate via:
• spring.profiles.active=prod in application.properties
• -Dspring.profiles.active=prod JVM arg
• SPRING_PROFILES_ACTIVE=prod env variable
• @ActiveProfiles in tests

Priority: application-{profile}.yml > application.yml (profile overrides base)
🔒 Security + Exception Handling
Auth/Authz flow:
1. Request hits SecurityFilterChain
2. JwtAuthFilter extracts + validates token
3. UserDetailsService.loadUserByUsername()
4. Sets SecurityContextHolder
5. @PreAuthorize("hasRole('ADMIN')") checks
6. Method executes if authorized
Global exception handling: 
@ControllerAdvice
public class GlobalExceptionHandler {

  @ExceptionHandler(ResourceNotFoundException.class)
  public ResponseEntity<?> handleNotFound(
        ResourceNotFoundException ex) {
    return ResponseEntity
      .status(HttpStatus.NOT_FOUND)
      .body(new ErrorResponse(ex.getMessage()));
  }
}

title: Microservices Patterns
description: Architecture patterns from your PDF — Gateway, Circuit Breaker, Saga, CQRS, and more.
bodyHtml:
GATEWAY
Must Know
API Gateway Pattern: Single entry point for all clients. Handles authentication, routing, rate limiting, load balancing, and response aggregation. Tools: Spring Cloud Gateway, AWS API Gateway, Kong.

Senior angle: Discuss BFF (Backend for Frontend) variant — separate gateway per client type (mobile vs web).
CIRCUIT BREAKER
Must Know
Circuit Breaker Pattern: Prevents cascading failures. States: CLOSED (normal) → OPEN (failing, reject requests) → HALF_OPEN (probe). Tool: Resilience4j.

Senior angle: "We added circuit breaker on our payment service call. When it opened, we returned cached pricing instead of failing the entire checkout."
SAGA
Important
Saga Pattern: Manages distributed transactions across services. Two variants:
• Choreography: Each service emits events (Kafka). Decoupled but hard to track.
• Orchestration: Central orchestrator (saga coordinator) drives the flow.

Compensating transactions handle rollback when a step fails.
CQRS
Important
CQRS (Command Query Responsibility Segregation): Separate read and write models. Writes go to Command service → write DB. Reads from Query service → read-optimized DB (maybe Redis/Elasticsearch).

Why? Read-heavy systems can scale reads independently. Write model can use domain events.
SERVICE REGISTRY
Important
Service Registry / Discovery: Services register themselves on startup. Clients look up service locations dynamically. Tool: Eureka (Netflix), Consul.

Client-side LB: Client queries registry + load balances (Ribbon, Spring Cloud LoadBalancer).
Server-side LB: Gateway does the lookup.
EVENT-DRIVEN
Important
Event-Driven Architecture: Services communicate via events (Kafka, RabbitMQ). Producer emits events, consumers react independently. Enables loose coupling and scalability.

Kafka key concepts: Topic, Partition, Consumer Group, Offset. Partitions enable parallelism. Consumer groups enable competing consumers.
DB PER SERVICE
Must Know
Database per Service: Each microservice owns its data store exclusively. No shared DB. Ensures loose coupling and independent deployment.

Challenge: Cross-service queries require API calls or event-driven denormalization. Transactions across services need Saga pattern.
STRANGLER FIG
Nice to Know
Strangler Fig Pattern: Gradually migrate a monolith to microservices by replacing components incrementally. A proxy routes traffic — old features still go to monolith, new features go to microservices until the monolith is "strangled."

Risk-free migration strategy for legacy systems.

title: SQL Practice
description: Classic interview SQL queries + indexing + optimization. All sourced from your question list.
bodyHtml:
Q: 2nd Highest Salary / Marks
-- Method 1: LIMIT + OFFSET (MySQL) SELECT salary FROM employees ORDER BY salary DESC LIMIT 1 OFFSET 1; -- Method 2: Subquery (cross-DB) SELECT MAX(salary) FROM employees WHERE salary < (SELECT MAX(salary) FROM employees); -- Method 3: DENSE_RANK (best for Nth) SELECT salary FROM ( SELECT salary, DENSE_RANK() OVER (ORDER BY salary DESC) AS rnk FROM employees ) t WHERE rnk = 2;
💡 Prefer DENSE_RANK for "Nth highest" — handles ties correctly. Ask interviewer: "Should ties count as one rank or separate?"
Q: Types of JOINs
-- INNER JOIN: only matching rows SELECT e.name, d.name FROM employees e INNER JOIN departments d ON e.dept_id = d.id; -- LEFT JOIN: all from left + matches SELECT e.name, d.name FROM employees e LEFT JOIN departments d ON e.dept_id = d.id; -- Find employees WITHOUT a department SELECT e.name FROM employees e LEFT JOIN departments d ON e.dept_id = d.id WHERE d.id IS NULL;
Q: Indexing Concepts
-- Create index CREATE INDEX idx_email ON users(email); -- Composite index (order matters!) CREATE INDEX idx_name_dept ON employees(dept_id, name); -- Check if query uses index EXPLAIN SELECT * FROM users WHERE email = 'x@y.com';
Types: B-Tree (default, range queries), Hash (exact match only), Full-text (text search)
When NOT to index: Low-cardinality columns (boolean, status with 2 values), small tables, write-heavy tables
Senior tip: "We analyzed EXPLAIN output and found a full-table scan on a 5M row table. Adding a composite index on (user_id, created_at) reduced query time from 8s to 40ms."
Q: Query Optimization
-- BAD: SELECT * (fetches all columns) SELECT * FROM orders WHERE status = 'PENDING'; -- GOOD: Specify needed columns SELECT id, user_id, total FROM orders WHERE status = 'PENDING'; -- BAD: Function on indexed column SELECT * FROM users WHERE YEAR(created_at) = 2024; -- GOOD: Range on indexed column SELECT * FROM users WHERE created_at BETWEEN '2024-01-01' AND '2024-12-31';
Q: Find Duplicate Records
SELECT email, COUNT(*) AS cnt FROM users GROUP BY email HAVING COUNT(*) > 1 ORDER BY cnt DESC;
Q: Store Procedures vs Functions
Stored Procedure: Can have IN/OUT/INOUT params. Can modify data. Called with CALL. Can return multiple result sets. No return type required.

Function: Must return a value. Can be used in SELECT. Usually read-only (can't do DML in most DBs). Pure computation.

Views vs Tables: Views are virtual tables (stored query). No data storage. Useful for security (expose subset of columns). Updatable only if single-table, no aggregation.
🔑 ACID Principles
A — AtomicityAll operations in a transaction succeed, or all are rolled back. No partial state.
C — ConsistencyDB moves from one valid state to another. All constraints satisfied after transaction.
I — IsolationConcurrent transactions don't see each other's intermediate state. Levels: READ COMMITTED, SERIALIZABLE.
D — DurabilityCommitted transactions persist even after system failures. Written to disk, not just memory.

title: Senior Developer Gap Analysis
description: JD vs current prep. Know exactly where to focus in your remaining time.
bodyHtml:
✅ Likely Strengths (based on your questions)
Spring Boot Annotations — You've clearly worked with @RestController, @Service, @Bean etc. extensively
REST API Design — @PathVariable, @RequestParam, @RequestBody, ResponseEntity all mentioned
Java 8 Basics — Lambda, streams, functional interfaces covered well
Collections — HashMap, ArrayList, LinkedList, Set — well-practiced
Exception Handling — @ControllerAdvice, @ExceptionHandler pattern known
JPA / ORM — Entity annotations, repo.save(), transactions covered
⚠️ Gaps vs Senior JD
Architecture Decisions — Can you articulate WHY you chose a design? Not just WHAT.
Production Troubleshooting — Memory leaks, thread dumps, high CPU, OOM — prepare real stories.
Multithreading Depth — Deadlock detection, volatile vs atomic, thread pool sizing.
Microservices Patterns — Circuit breaker, Saga, CQRS — know when and why to use each.
Performance Optimization — Query optimization, caching strategy, N+1 problem.
Docker / Kubernetes basics — JD lists as "Important." Dockerfile, basic K8s concepts.
Mentoring Stories — "Tell me about a time you mentored a junior dev or led a code review."
🎯 Top 5 Things to Nail for This Role
1. Design Ownership Story
Prepare one story: "I designed [X] system. The requirements were [Y]. I chose [architecture] because [reasons]. The tradeoffs were [Z]. In production, we handled [issue] by [solution]."
2. Scalability Thinking
For any answer, add "At scale, the concern would be X, so we'd add caching/pagination/async processing." This signals senior-level thinking immediately.
3. Microservices Pattern Depth
Know Gateway, Circuit Breaker, and DB-per-service cold. Be able to draw these on a whiteboard and explain when you'd use Saga vs 2PC.
4. MySQL Optimization
Know EXPLAIN output, B-tree vs hash indexes, the N+1 problem with JPA, and how you'd tune slow queries. Mention a real performance fix you did.
5. Leadership + Code Review Examples
The JD explicitly mentions "mentor junior developers and conduct code reviews." Prepare 2 examples: (a) a time you improved a junior's code during review, (b) a time you set a coding standard or pattern for the team.

title: Revision Plan
description: Your interview is next week. Here's a focused day-by-day plan.
bodyHtml:
📅 Day 1 (Today)Must Know
☕ Core Java: HashMap internals, Collections, String immutability
🔄 Multithreading: volatile, synchronized, deadlock, thread lifecycle
🚀 Java 8: Streams, Lambdas, Optional — practice 5 stream queries
📝 Write down your 3 key project stories (STAR format)
📅 Day 2Must Know
🍃 Spring Boot: All annotations, DI, Bean scopes, Profiles, Actuator
🌐 REST APIs: Best practices, HTTP methods, status codes, validation
⚡ Caching: @Cacheable flow, Redis basics
🔒 Security: JWT auth flow, @ControllerAdvice exception handling
📅 Day 3Must Know
🔗 Microservices: Gateway, Circuit Breaker, DB per service, Kafka
🏗️ Design Patterns: Singleton, Factory, Observer, Strategy, Builder
🧬 OOP + SOLID: All 5 principles with Java examples
🗄️ SQL: Joins, 2nd highest, indexing, EXPLAIN, ACID
📅 Day 4Important
🎯 Mock Interview: Do 20 questions cold — no peeking at answers first
💬 Practice STAR stories aloud for all 6 story prep scenarios
🔍 Review weak areas identified in Mock mode
☁️ Docker basics: dockerfile, docker-compose, K8s pod/service/deployment
📅 Day 5Important
🎯 Full mock interview: 30 questions, timed (1.5 min each)
📊 Senior-level Q&A: architecture decisions, tradeoffs, production issues
🔁 Re-read all cheat sheets
📝 Prepare 3 questions to ASK the interviewer (shows seniority)
📅 Day BeforeLight
⚡ Cheat sheet only — no new topics
🧘 Rest. Sleep 8 hours. No cramming.
✅ Quick review of your 3 STAR stories
📋 Re-read the JD. Map your experience to each requirement.
🎤 3 Questions to Ask Your Interviewer
1. "What does the current architecture look like, and what are the biggest technical challenges the team is working through?"
2. "How does the team handle code reviews and technical decision-making? Is there a tech lead or are decisions collaborative?"
3. "What does success look like in the first 90 days for this role?"

title: Project & Experience Story Prep
description: STAR format stories for behavioral questions. Personalize with your real project details.
bodyHtml:
🏗️ "Walk me through an API you designed"
Also: "How do you design a REST API?" / "Tell me about your REST API best practices"
Situation
"In my project, we needed a REST API for [feature — e.g., user order management]. The frontend team needed real-time data while we also had mobile clients."
Task
"I was responsible for designing the API contract, versioning strategy, and ensuring it was backward-compatible."
Action
"I used RESTful conventions: resource-based URIs (/orders/{id}), proper HTTP verbs, JSON payloads with validation (@Valid + ConstraintViolation handling), paginated list responses, and ResponseEntity for proper status codes. I added @ControllerAdvice for centralized error handling. I used Spring profiles to point to different DB configs in dev/staging/prod."
Result
"The API was consumed by 3 clients. No breaking changes in 2 releases. Avg response time was under 100ms."
⚡ "Tell me about a performance issue you solved"
Also: "How do you handle slow queries?" / "N+1 problem"
Situation
"Our order list API was taking 8+ seconds in production with 100k+ records. Users were reporting timeouts."
Action
"I ran EXPLAIN on the slow query and found a full table scan due to no index on the status column. I also discovered an N+1 issue in JPA — for each order, it was making a separate DB call to load the user. I fixed it with: (1) Added composite index on (status, created_at). (2) Used @EntityGraph or JOIN FETCH to load user with orders in one query. (3) Added @Cacheable on frequently-read reference data."
Result
"Response time dropped from 8s to 180ms. DB query count per request went from 50+ to 2. No more timeout errors."
🔒 "How did you implement security in your project?"
Also: "Authentication vs Authorization" / "How does JWT work?"
Action
"We used Spring Security with JWT. On login, we generated a signed JWT with user ID, roles, and expiry. Every subsequent request carried the JWT in the Authorization header. A custom JwtAuthFilter validated the token before the request reached the controller. Role-based access used @PreAuthorize('hasRole(\"ADMIN\")'). Passwords were hashed with BCrypt. We also added CORS config and rate limiting to prevent abuse."
Senior addition
"We also implemented refresh tokens to keep sessions alive without asking users to re-login frequently, and had a token blacklist (Redis) for logout to handle token invalidation."
💥 "Tell me about a production issue you debugged"
Framework
Structure: Symptoms → Hypothesis → Investigation → Root cause → Fix → Prevention

Example: "We had intermittent 503 errors during peak traffic. I checked the logs — thread pool was saturated. Using JMC + thread dumps, I found threads stuck waiting for DB connections. Our connection pool (HikariCP) was too small for peak load. I increased pool size from 10 to 30, added connection timeout config, and added @Async processing for non-critical tasks to reduce sync DB load. Added Actuator metrics to alert on pool exhaustion."
💾 "@Transactional — Tell me how you used it"
Answer
"@Transactional on a service method wraps the entire method in a DB transaction — all operations succeed or all rollback. I used it on our order creation service: insert order → deduct inventory → charge payment. If any step failed, the transaction rolled back automatically via Spring's declarative transaction management (AOP proxy).

Gotchas I hit: (1) @Transactional doesn't work on private methods — the AOP proxy can't intercept them. (2) If you call a @Transactional method from within the same class, it bypasses the proxy. Solution: inject the bean into itself or restructure. (3) For rollback on checked exceptions, you need: @Transactional(rollbackFor = Exception.class) — by default only RuntimeExceptions trigger rollback."
👥 "How do you approach code reviews / mentoring?"
Answer
"In code reviews, I focus on three layers: correctness (does it work?), design (is it maintainable?), and craft (is it readable?). I leave inline comments with the 'why' not just the 'what' — e.g., instead of 'use HashMap here,' I'd write 'HashMap gives O(1) lookup here vs O(n) for this List.contains() call.' For junior devs, I follow up in person to make sure the feedback landed. I also try to call out things done well — code reviews shouldn't be only critique."
